items.mistake: Treating every interactive system as reinforcement learning
items.fix: Reserve RL for problems with sequential decisions, a policy to learn, and a reward (or return) to optimize. Rule-based chatbots or supervised rankers are not RL unless reward-driven policy learning is central.
items.why: Overusing the RL label obscures simpler solutions and hides missing ingredients (state, action, reward).
items.example: A spam filter trained on labeled emails is supervised classification, not RL—even though it runs online.

items.fix: Labels tell the model the correct output; rewards score outcomes and may not identify a unique best action at each step.
items.why: Applying supervised loss to RL problems ignores delayed credit assignment and exploration requirements.
items.example: In chess, only the final win/loss is certain—the move that led to victory 40 plies earlier is not a direct label for that timestep.

items.mistake: Pure exploitation from step one (epsilon = 0 always)
items.fix: Keep deliberate exploration early (epsilon-greedy, entropy bonus, or structured randomization) until value estimates are reliable.
items.why: Greedy policies on untrained value estimates lock onto suboptimal actions and never discover better options.
items.example: A bandit agent that always picks arm 0 after one lucky win may miss arm 1 with true rate 0.9.

items.mistake: Ignoring discount factor γ when rewards are delayed
items.fix: Choose γ to reflect how far ahead the task cares about outcomes; verify return calculations use consistent discounting.
items.why: γ = 0 makes the agent myopic; γ near 1 with noisy rewards increases variance in return estimates.
items.example: With γ = 0.99, a +1 reward 100 steps away contributes 0.99^100 ≈ 0.37 to the return—not the full 1.0.
